Title: Stack
Description: A linear data structure that follows the Last In First Out (LIFO) principle — the last element pushed is the first one to be popped.
Explanation:
A Stack is one of the most fundamental data structures in computer science. It operates on the **LIFO** principle — Last In, First Out. Think of it like a stack of plates: you can only add or remove a plate from the top.

A Stack supports four core operations:

**Push:** Add a new element to the top of the stack. If the stack has a fixed capacity and is full, this results in a stack overflow.

**Pop:** Remove and return the element at the top of the stack. If the stack is empty, this results in a stack underflow error.

**Peek (Top):** Return the top element without removing it. Useful when you need to inspect the top value before deciding whether to pop.

**Reset (Clear):** Remove all elements from the stack, returning it to an empty state.

**Real-world uses of Stack:**
- **Function call stack:** Every time a function is called, its frame is pushed onto the call stack. When it returns, it is popped.
- **Undo/Redo:** Text editors push every action onto a stack. Ctrl+Z pops the last action.
- **Expression evaluation:** Compilers use stacks to evaluate arithmetic expressions and match parentheses.
- **Browser history:** The back button pops the current page off a history stack.

**Two common implementations:**
- **Array-based:** Fixed size, O(1) push/pop, but limited capacity.
- **Linked List-based:** Dynamic size, O(1) push/pop, no capacity limit.
